QFIN's PEG ratio is another key highlight. Qifu's PEG Non-GAAP (FWD) ratio of 0.35 again reflects that it is undervalued, and you'll pay less for each unit of the earnings, making it an attractive option in the consumer finance sector. Revenue growth is also pretty decent, in my opinion, with a 3-year CAGR of 5.29% and a 5-year CAGR of 19.19%. I think that in a sector where most of its peers are struggling, QFIN's revenue potential reflects its healthy growth, and it is a positive sign of its viability in the long run.

What I like about QFIN is its solid levered FCF margin of 12.62%, which is higher than the majority of its peers. Also, its return on equity of 21.98% further demonstrates operational efficiency, showcasing growth and profitability.

Overall, I believe QFIN offers a solid growth opportunity at a much lower price point, making it a strong buy.

Valuation

QFIN stock has surged significantly in the past one year and the reasons are obvious, its strong fundamentals. Due to this, the market has respond with great enthusiasm, taking the stock to $31.33 with an increase of 108% over the past one year.

In my opinion, QFIN is undervalued based on valuation metrics. It has a P/E GAAP (TTM) ratio of 8.00 which is nearly 40% lower than the sector median and strengthens my bullish case. This, combined with its P/E GAAP(FWD) of 6.25, reflects that the market has not fully recognized QFIN's future earnings potential. I think PEG GAAP (TTM) of 0.31 further indicates that QFIN is priced attractively relative to its growth prospects, with a 44% discount compared to the sector.

The forward EV/EBITDA and forward EV/EBIT ratios show that QFIN is trading low relative to its sector, with values of 4.49 and 4.52, respectively, both more than 65% lower than sector medians. In my opinion, QFIN is delivering strong earnings with efficient capital allocation; hence the ratios speak for themselves. Its price/sales (FWD) ratio of 2.20 is also 25% lower than that of the sector, showing that the market is not adequately pricing its revenue potential.

Finally, the dividend yield of 3.58% is above the sector median, which is a nice bonus if you are an income-focused investor. Given these undervaluation metrics, QFIN is a clear buy, in my opinion, with both growth and income potential.

EPS estimates for the full year are expected to be around $5.28 reflecting a significant growth of 39.14%. Next year's estimate is $5.61 with a modest increase of 6.4%. Similarly, revenue estimates for FY24 are $2.33 billion and with YoY growth of 2.66%. Next year, it is expected to increase at 2.71% to reach $2.39 billion.

Why am I Bullish on QFIN?

So, here is what my verdict on QFIN, yes, it's the right time to buy. Not only its valuation but also the company's business model and macroeconomic changes drive my bullish case.

The company is in the digital lending business, targeting young urban Chinese with white collar corporate jobs. The company is poised to increase its market share and customer base in China, and we can see that efforts in the earnings growth in Q2 and hopefully Q3 and Q4 will follow the same. I think this will boost its FCFs and will help the company to deliver value to its shareholders with strong dividends and stock buybacks.

Also, I think that on the macroeconomic scale, the recent changes in Chinese policies will help the business to take advantage of it.

QFIN's profitability metrics are another reason why I am bullish on the stock. Its EBIT margin of 45.27% and EBITDA margin of 45.69% are more than double the sector median, which shows the operating efficiency of the company, meaning it is efficient in converting revenues into profits. The net income margin of 28.08% is also a standout, outperforming competitors by 25%, highlighting solid profitability in a sector that has extreme volatility. This means that the company is efficient in recovering the cash it lends to people, thus increasing its profits. While the gross profit margin of 60.69% is slightly ahead of the sector, the return on equity (22.12%) and return on total capital (21.46%) are excellent. Despite a slightly lower free cash flow margin, QFIN's profitability makes it a compelling buy, in my opinion.

Risks

Being a digital lending company, QFIN has several risks. Some risks are related to the tech side, but one risk that any lending company (digital or conventional) will have is its borrowers not being able to pay the money borrowed. And especially in a digital lending business where they lend money without any collateral, it is a serious risk. As we have discussed, the company uses AI, ML, and big data to study its customers, but life is still unpredictable, and any change in the customers' lives, like losing a job, etc., could affect Qifu's revenues.

Another threat is that any tech business would have cybersecurity threats, and any breach could be a serious blow in terms of compromising customers' data, etc.

A policy change on the state level is another risk that could affect Qifu's business.

Conclusion

Qifu Technology, in my opinion, is an underrated stock in the consumer finance sector. The company has a strong balance sheet, steady growth, maintains profitability, and offers consistent dividends. It has all the qualities one could ask for in a stock. Though the risks are significant, QFIN, being a solid credit-tech company, knows how to take calculated risks with its robust system. If you want to diversify your portfolio and looking for an attractive investment in the consumer finance sector, QFIN is the best stock for you.

data.title: SMH: Promising, But I'm Waiting For A More Attractive Price
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend holding the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH). There is no doubt that the semiconductor segment is promising, and should bring companies a strong increase in productivity of between 0.1% and 0.6% per year until 2040, with the automation of 60% to 70% of employee activities.

This expectation is corroborated by the significant investment that large technology companies are making. However, one of the great attractions of an ETF is the reduction of risks specific to each company, and currently, there is no margin of safety even with the diversification that VanEck provides.

VanEck Semiconductor ETF

Before we talk about the investment thesis, I would like to introduce you to the VanEck Semiconductor ETF. The ETF currently invests in 24 stocks of companies that operate in the production and development of semiconductors, as we can see below.

The ETF seeks to replicate the performance of companies in the semiconductor segment, seeking to follow the performance of the MVIS US Listed Semiconductor 25 Index, promoting diversification, liquidity and practicality for investors seeking exposure to the segment.

Productivity Gains With AI

I recently wrote an article about the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), where I listed how promising the semiconductor segment was based mainly on geopolitical issues, but in this article, I intend to address other topics.

I will bring studies on the productivity gains that AI should promote for large technology companies and how these strategic investments can become great competitive advantages for these companies.

McKinsey recently published studies on the productivity gains that generative AI would promote in the economy. What caught my attention the most was that the company projects that generative AI should enable an increase in productivity of 0.1% to 0.6% per year until 2040.

However, combined with other technologies, this productivity gain could be 0.5% to 3.4% per year until 2040. This would result in the automation of 60% to 70% of workers' activities, requiring training and reallocation. These projections seem very pertinent, as we will see the investments of big tech companies below.

Investments By Large Companies In Semiconductors Have Soared

One of the most assertive ways to analyze whether a trend has the potential to be structural is to check how much capital is being invested in it, and we can see in the graph below how the big technology players are making a lot of capex in semiconductors, especially for infrastructure related to AI.

This trend can also be seen in the evolution of American industrial production when we analyze three sectors: Computer equipment, communications equipment and semiconductors. Let's look at the graph below.

We can see a clear rise in the semiconductor sector post-pandemic, especially after the announcement of the CHIPS Act and the injection of many stimuli for the American semiconductor industry. But with all these great signs, why my skeptical view and recommendation to hold the ETF?

Valuation Has No Safety Margin

One of the great benefits of ETFs is convenience and security, since diversification mitigates the specific risks of each company. However, when we look at the ETF prospectus itself, we see that the P/E and P/B are extremely high, which already indicates that the asset is not cheap.

In my view, one of the best alternatives to analyzing the valuation of a growth company ETF is by analyzing its PEG. To do this, I will use a tool to analyze the ETF's PEG against its own history.

Since the semiconductor market is extremely cyclical, I will use the horizon of the last three years. As we can see, in the last 3 years the ETF has traded at a minimum of 1.4x PEG and a maximum of 3.4x PEG, making an average of 2.4x PEG in the last 3 years.

But as we can see from the chart, it currently trades at a PEG of 2.5x, which implies a 4% downside to return to the historical average of the last 3 years. Based on this, I recommend holding the VanEck Semiconductor ETF since there is no margin of safety for the investment.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

As threats to my skeptical thesis regarding the VanEck Semiconductor ETF, we can say that it is not a consensus, since Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend a strong buy, as we can see below.

On the other hand, as threats to an optimistic thesis, we can mention that the VanEck Semiconductor ETF presents greater risks than the iShares Semiconductor ETF, since VanEck has 24 invested companies, while iShares has 30 invested stocks.

The investment thesis is complex since it covers many stocks, and the sector is characterized by being cyclical and expectations regarding the company's results being quite volatile, so the investor should be cautious when conducting their studies.

The Bottom Line

A great way to corroborate a "great promise" of investment is to understand how much is being invested in it. Unlike themes that are falling into oblivion, such as ESG investments, we could see in this article that investments in semiconductors and AI are very heated by large technology companies.

Although I believe this is a promising segment, as an analyst who follows the value investing philosophy, I do not see enough safety margin in the PEG, P/E, and P/B metrics to recommend buying this ETF.

Based on this analysis, I recommend holding the VanEck Semiconductor ETF. In my view, investors should be patient and wait for a more attractive risk/return ratio to invest in this interesting ETF.

data.title: John Wiley & Sons: (Still) Less Compelling Than Treasuries
data.description:
It’s been about four months since I suggested that John Wiley & Sons (NYSE:WLYB) was less compelling than Treasury Notes. Since then, the shares have returned about 28.7% against a gain of about 8% for the S&P 500. My caution has cost me yet again, apparently. The company has posted financial results since, so it’s time to review the name yet again. I’m going to review the latest financials, and also review the valuation. It may be the case that a stock trading for $49.62 is more compelling an investment than when it was trading at $39.14. This all depends on the financial results, and the relative returns you might earn from Treasuries.

Welcome to the thesis statement portion of the program, where I offer you the gist of what I’m thinking so you can relieve yourself of the potential tedium of reading the whole piece. You’re welcome. In spite of the fact that there were some improvements to the financial results, I’m going to remain on the sidelines. If I were deploying new capital, I’d certainly choose 10 Year Treasuries over these shares for a few reasons. First, there were some not so bright financial spots. Second, the valuation is quite high in my view. The last time the shares traded at current levels, they went on to underperform. Given that you can earn more money, more predictably, with Treasury Notes suggests that investment in these shares makes no sense. Additionally, we should remember that along with being more predictable, Treasury Notes offer capital gain potential, too.

Financial Snapshot

In some ways, the most recent financial results were fairly impressive. In spite of a 10.5% drop in sales from the same period last year, operating income swung from a $16.4 million loss last year to a positive $29 million this year. This drove net income swinging from a $92.3 million loss last year to basically breaking even this year. Fully $47.8 million, of this improvement, comes directly from much lower cost of sales. The company also benefited from restructuring charges that were $8.25 million lower, and operating and administrative expenses that were $6.9 million lower. The income statement paints a picture of an impressive turnaround.

It’s not all animated bluebirds and lollipops, though. The capital structure has deteriorated, with long-term debt up to ~$918.55 million, which is a decade high for the firm by a very wide margin. Specifically, the company left FY 2024 with debt of about $774.5, so it’s up about 18.6% over the quarter. Interest expense is up about 12.9% over the same period a year ago, and I think this figure will grow at a faster rate going forward.

Additionally, things look less rosy when we look at the cash flow statement. Cash from operations was a loss of $88.7 million during the most recent quarter, relative to a loss of about $82.3 million during the same period last year.

Things have certainly improved at the company, but in my view, the reality is that “less bad” is still “bad.” For that reason, I’d expect to see a relatively compelling valuation.

The Stock

In the domain of investing, everything is relative, and the future matters more than the past. So, if you buy one stock, you are, by definition, not applying that capital to a host of other stocks or other investment opportunities. Additionally, my excess of caution a few months ago meant that I missed out on some of the gains here. While I could argue that the Notes I did buy offer a level of predictability that these shares lack until I’m blue in the face, there’s no getting around the fact that my caution cost me. That should not taint my analysis now, though. I need to focus on whether the shares are cheap at the moment. In particular, I want to determine whether or not they’re cheap relative to the risks and opportunities present here.

I think it’s worth noting that when I reviewed this name previously, the 10 Year Treasury Note was yielding 4.228% and the dividend on the stock was 3.5% (.728). Today the 10 Year Note sits at 4.057%, and the dividend yield is 2.8% (1.25%). Using the arithmetic skills not so lovingly beaten into me by the good sisters at Holy Spirit School years ago, I have determined that, as the stock has climbed in price, the relative spread between the Treasury and the stock has blown out by 71%. This makes shares far less relatively attractive in my view.

That’s not enough to disqualify the shares, though. If they’re otherwise cheap enough, it might be worth buying the shares. Those who read my stuff regularly know that I apply a few metrics to decide if a stock is trading cheaply enough or not. Some of these are more complex than others. On the simple side, I look at the relationship between price and some measure of economic value like sales, or earnings, and the like. The lower the price per unit of economic value, the better. In particular, I want to see shares trading at a discount both to the overall market and to their own history. On this score, I think it's fair to say, at ~1.49 times sales, the current valuation of John Wiley is rather high. I think it’s worth noting that the last time the shares were trading at current levels, they went on to underperform. Additionally, I think it’s a mistake for the market to pay $1.49 for $1 of future sales for a company that’s still in the red, even if things are improving.

In addition to looking at simple ratios, I want to try to understand what the market is currently "thinking" about a given company's future. In order to do this, I turn to the work of Stephen Penman, particularly his book "Accounting for Value." One of the most interesting ideas expressed in this book is that share price is itself an interesting source of information, and gives us clues about what the market view of the future of the business is. So, if the market’s assumptions are too optimistic, that’s bad.

We get at the assumptions by applying some high school algebra to work out the “g” (growth) variable in a standard finance formula. The greater the expectations, the more risky the investment. According to this approach, the market currently "thinks" that John Wiley & Sons will grow at a rate of about 11.5% from current levels. In my view, that is excessive, and is yet another reason to avoid this stock at current levels.

At the risk of “droning on” about the negative risk premium here, a risk I’m very much willing to take obviously, an investor can earn more income, more predictably, with a Treasury Note than they would with this stock over the next decade. That, as Americans say, “is not nothing.” To those who would argue that the stock may provide a capital gain, I would remind them that Treasury Notes can do the same.

Given all of the above, I think prudence dictates that I remain on the sidelines here. The shares may continue to rise from current levels, but that’s not as relevant to me as the risk that an investor takes on at current levels to achieve those returns. As I’ve written before, the market giveth, the market taketh awayeth.
